Affectations: mannerisms, behaviors, speech patterns deviating from norm. Manifest in: speech, gestures, diction, dress/appearance, interactions, vocabulary. Everyone has some, intentional or natural. Adopted via environment, family, culture, education.

Couples develop shared affectations over time. Spouses adopt each other's affectations for: fitting in, pleasing partner, effective communication, showing respect. Changes faster in shorter relationships depending on dynamics.

Businesses have common employee affectations from: company culture, professional norms, training. Shared affectations appear outside workplace (social events, online). May go unnoticed in homogenous groups.

Narrative pitch: couple develops shared affectations over marriage; one spouse's affectations reset to their original state (from marriage start). Ideas: career change, life event, illness, travel, job loss impact on relationship dynamics.

Quick-adoption affectations: speech patterns (accent, colloquialisms), gestures, body language, diction (pronunciation, word usage), dress/appearance, interaction styles, vocabulary, colloquial phrases.

Gesture affectations: hand gestures, facial expressions, posture, eye contact, touch (handshake, hug), proximity (standing distance), head movements (nodding, tilting). Vary by culture/context. Quickly adopted in new environments.

Narrative: person adopts others' affectations to fit in but feels ridiculed instead. Examples: new employee mocked by colleagues; student abroad teased; new city resident dismissed; friend-group newcomer excluded; co-workers laugh instead of accept.

Narrative: employee leaves 1mo for different job, returns with new affectations (speech, dress, behavior). Coworkers remember them differently, struggle to accept changes, feel distanced. Affects work relationships, adjustment difficulty.

Le Havre: historic industrial architecture impacts tourism positively. UNESCO World Heritage Site (modernist architecture by Auguste Perret). Preserved docks (Vauban), warehouses, industrial sites attract architecture/history tourists.

Le Havre: Docks Vauban renovated (cultural/commercial center, shops, restaurants). Maison de l'Armateur (maritime museum). La Forge Museum (industrial heritage). Demonstrates commitment to preserving industrial heritage.

Le Havre attractions: Auguste Perret architecture tour, Maison de l'Armateur (18th-century warehouse), Docks Vauban (refurbished complex), André Malraux Museum (modern art, Niemeyer building), La Forge Museum, Saint Joseph Church (Perret, tallest spire France), MuMa impressionist art.

Le Havre restaurants: Les Enfants Sages (French/Norman), Le Bouchon Normand (traditional Norman), La Civette (seafood), Le Chat Bleu (classic French), L'Écailler (fresh seafood). Traditional regional cuisine focus.

User organized kitchen utensil drawer recently. Now researching countertop materials/styles for island. Trending materials: quartz, butcher block, marble, recycled glass, concrete. Styles: waterfall edge, mixed materials, live edge, monochromatic, pattern play.

Butcher block pros: easy install/repair, sand/refinish if damaged, warm look, affordable. Cons: requires maintenance, scratches/dents/cuts prone, bacteria risk, periodic re-oil/wax. Daily: wipe spills, clean with mild soap, apply oil 1-2x/week.

Granite pros: extremely durable, resistant scratches/heat/stains, low maintenance, increases home value, many colors/patterns. Cons: expensive, requires sealing, etches over time, heavy (needs support). Both good options, user leaning butcher block.
